title: Inside the Race to Protect Ukrainian
gist: Ukraine treats language protection as a national-security instrument as much as a cultural one, running a 2019 state-language law with light enforcement domestically while documenting Russian destruction of Ukrainian-language education and media in occupied territories to build an international 'language genocide' case.

themes.theme: Language as a front of the war

insights.claim: Western media's mass shift from 'Kiev' to 'Kyiv' after Russia's February 2022 invasion shows how a geopolitical shock can force a rapid, coordinated change in a foreign language's naming conventions, even affecting product names like British supermarkets' 'chicken Kyiv.'
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: Naming conventions in Western media are a lagging but trackable indicator of which side of a conflict international opinion has aligned with.
insights.locator: Even if you aren't Ukrainian, it is still likely that you took part

insights.claim: Wherever Russia has occupied Ukrainian territory since 2022, it has dismantled Ukrainian-language road signs, imposed Russian as the language of instruction and public life, and seized or destroyed Ukrainian books.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: Systematic language suppression in occupied zones is a concrete, documentable input into Ukraine's international legal case against Russia.
insights.locator: In the meantime, Russia has steamrolled through Ukrainian sovereign territory

insights.claim: Ukraine's 2019 state language law was pushed hard by politicians for security reasons as much as cultural ones, framing language policy explicitly as a national-defense tool rather than purely a heritage-preservation measure.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: Language legislation in Ukraine should be read as part of its security apparatus, relevant to any analysis of Ukrainian state cohesion under continued war.
insights.locator: The state language law that created Taras Kremin's job was introduced in 2019

themes.theme: The Ukrainian state language law, in practice

insights.claim: Ukraine's 2019 state language law (49 pages) requires proficiency in Ukrainian for politicians, military officers, bank staff, state education and medicine workers, and lawyers, and requires new citizens to demonstrate proficiency immediately or within a year of acquiring citizenship.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: The law functions as a broad civic-integration mandate touching finance, medicine, and law, not just education, with compliance costs for a wide range of institutions operating in Ukraine.
insights.locator: An enormous number of professions require proficiency in Ukrainian

insights.claim: Publishers operating in Ukraine must produce at least 50 percent of their titles in Ukrainian, and foreign websites serving Ukrainian users must load in Ukrainian by default.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: Any foreign media or e-commerce company serving Ukrainian users faces a hard localization compliance requirement enforced under this law.
insights.locator: Publishers are obliged to produce at least 50 percent

insights.claim: Russian lost its status as a protected minority language in Ukraine in 2025, while Indigenous and EU-recognized minority languages such as Tatar retain protected status under the same law.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: The 2025 delisting marks a formal legal downgrade of Russian's status in Ukraine, a concrete marker of accelerating de-Russification distinct from wartime rhetoric.
insights.locator: Russian does not benefit, having had its status as a protected language removed

insights.claim: Despite its broad scope, enforcement of Ukraine's language law is light: only 64 fines were issued in the year to October 2024, mostly for websites not being accessible in Ukrainian, and the Language Commissioner explicitly rejects a policing role.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: The law is closer to a soft compliance and documentation regime than an aggressive crackdown, which should temper narratives that frame it as harsh linguistic nationalism.
insights.quote: I want to underline: I am not a policeman. We collect this information for the next steps.
insights.locator: As Kremin puts it, he's not laying down the law

insights.claim: In Russian-annexed Crimea, the number of pupils studying Ukrainian in school collapsed from more than 12,000 before annexation to just 214 in the 2020-21 academic year, with only one school left teaching the language.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: A precise, cited enrollment collapse gives investors and policymakers a concrete metric for tracking cultural-erasure claims in occupied Crimea.
insights.locator: In Crimea, the number of pupils studying Ukrainian at school

insights.claim: The International Court of Justice ordered Russia in 2019 to preserve Ukrainians' right to Ukrainian-language education in Crimea, but the article states nothing changed as a result of that ruling.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the practical non-enforceability of ICJ rulings against Russia, relevant to expectations for any future international legal remedies.
insights.locator: In 2019, the International Court of Justice ordered Russia

insights.claim: In Donetsk and Luhansk, Russian was declared the state language soon after the 2014 occupation, and by 2017 all schools had switched the language of instruction to Russian, leaving graduates unable to enter Ukrainian universities without supplemental private Ukrainian tutoring.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: A generation of students in occupied Donbas faces a structural barrier to reintegration into Ukrainian civil society even in a hypothetical post-war reunification scenario.
insights.quote: The children themselves say: Rather than learning Ukrainian like this, it is better not to teach it at school at all.
insights.locator: Svitlana, a woman in Luhansk, told Deutsche Welle

insights.claim: An activist in occupied Crimea reported that speaking Ukrainian publicly now draws mockery or disdain from residents and visitors from Russia, describing the language's day-to-day suppression as a form of social punishment beyond formal legal restrictions.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: Social stigmatization, not just legal restriction, is part of the on-the-ground suppression mechanism in occupied Crimea.
insights.quote: the oppression of the Ukrainian language in Crimea can now be seen in everything. If you communicate in Ukrainian, some people laugh, others look at you with disdain, especially visitors from Russia.
insights.locator: Larysa Kitayska, an activist who was arrested

insights.claim: There is no formal international legal crime of 'linguicide,' but Ukraine's language-violation documentation is intended to support its broader argument that Russia is committing genocide, to be raised in international legal forums.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: Language-rights documentation functions as evidentiary infrastructure for Ukraine's genocide claims, not a standalone legal remedy.
insights.locator: There is no international crime of linguicide

insights.claim: As of February 2025, there were 9,264 individual applications against Russia at the European Convention on Human Rights from citizens in Crimea and other occupied areas, and in June 2024 the ECHR found Russia guilty 'beyond a reasonable doubt' of systematic rights violations in Crimea, even though Russia is no longer an ECHR member and does not comply with its rulings.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: ECHR rulings against Russia carry no direct enforcement power but compound reputational damage that can feed into sanctions and diplomatic isolation.
insights.locator: In June 2024, the European Convention on Human Rights

themes.theme: Domestic enforcement geography and disinformation risk

insights.claim: Kyiv receives the most language-law complaints of any Ukrainian city, 706 in the year reported (39 percent of the national total), because it absorbs refugees from Russian-speaking regions like Donetsk, Luhansk, Kherson, and Mykolaiv, followed by Odesa (326) and Kharkiv (149).
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: Internal displacement is reshaping Ukraine's linguistic enforcement geography, concentrating friction in Kyiv rather than the frontline regions themselves.
insights.locator: Ukraine's open data service shared a report in October 2024

insights.claim: Ukrainians get more of their news from social media than any of the other 24 European countries surveyed by the Reuters Institute in 2024, a reliance driven by wartime destruction of traditional broadcast infrastructure, which the article frames as a major information-war vulnerability that Ukraine's website-language rules are partly meant to address.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: Ukraine's heavy social-media news dependence is a structural exposure to Russian-language disinformation that outlasts any single legal fix.
insights.locator: In 2024, the Reuters Institute for the Study of Journalism reported
